# Transcrição: 04-Report Setup & Analysis- Top Level KPIs

## [00:00 - 03:01]

So what I wanna show you here is how I look at my numbers in ads manager. So this specific video will show you how I'm looking at different levels of analysis for an e-com account. So the first thing is the reporting for the different views of your data is in this section here. So you can see right now I can see the kind of campaign names, delivery, base strategy, et cetera. They are default columns when you log into as manager, if you've not set any customizations up. This is by default what Facebook give you. Now, if I'm looking at how well my e-com performance has been doing, I look at this and I kinda look at results. Okay, that's quite useful, reach, whatever, whatever. But if you look down, there's not really much decision-making I can make from this. So what I would do is to go into customizing my columns and creating an order for the data that makes more sense for me. So this is an interface that Facebook change every two to four weeks to add new metrics, new methodologies to look at your data. But this is kind of where it is right now. So you've got different areas of data that you can look at, clicks, awareness, messaging, et cetera. You can see what's been default selected here in the middle column. And on the right-hand side are the columns and the ordering as you'll see in AntsManager. So that's all great. Now, what I wanna show you here is how I set up my top level KPIs. So KPIs are key performance indicator. When I talk about KPIs, they are the high level numbers that speak to me. So now that I've set up this view here, I can now see some kind of insight. So there's a difference between reporting and insight. Reporting is just sharing numbers, for example, or knowledge or something. Insights is actually telling me a story, some kind of narrative of what's going on. So now that I've selected my top level KPIs and I explain what's in there, I can see my amount spent. I can see budget here, which is useful. But now I can see some additional data. So my website return on ad spend. So I can see at a glance, which campaigns are doing well, which are not. I can look at my cost per purchase, my result rate, which is the conversion rate from click to purchase. Or actually, one thing to just clarify, result rate is based on what objective you're optimizing for. So if my optimization is for video views, page engagement, link clicks, the result rate is connected to that. So it's not just pure sales conversion rates, just make sure that you know that. Then I'm looking at my cost per outbound click, my purchase conversion value. And I can see purchases. Now, if you've been through the training on understanding KPIs, and you'll then know what I'm looking for.

## [03:01 - 06:03]

So for example, this is Ecom, my lead metric in my ad account here is return on ad spend. For this particular ad account, over a 30 day period, we are trying to aim for two or above. So that's okay. On a daily basis, slightly lower, but there's an attribution that kicks in. So I can see that we're kind of doing okay here. 2.3 based on this spend. Our cost per acquisition is good. Cost per outbound click is okay. And we can see our purchase conversion value. Now, one thing about reading data is the devil is in the detail. And what I mean by that is, I could look at this and say, okay, we're okay, but I will get a better idea of what's going on once I understand what my benchmark is, or at least my baseline numbers. And then I compare my campaigns, my ad sets, and my ads to those benchmarks. So if I look at this period, so this is 30 days, my average return on ad spend is 2.33. So for every dollar I spend, I get $2.33 back. But if I scan across here, I can see how that looks across my funnel. So at the moment it's listed based on ad spend. So I can see, for example, for this particular campaign, most of our budget has gone into the warm audience. We're doing a lot of prospecting and kind of generating leads to then bring into our warm audience. And that's where majority of the spend is coming from. And at that ROAS, it's doing okay, but it's not the best ROAS, which is coming from our retargeting. For this one, it's a catalog, Dynamic Products Catalog Ad. And I can see here, I'm getting a ROAS of 4.7. And then I scan across some of these. I can see 1.34, four, our existing customers are lower than I'd want. It's a three ROAS. My cold is 1.4. Another retargeting campaign, which we more recently launched, is doing much better, for example. So I'm scanning down to spot opportunities. Like the whole point of getting a dashboard set up, and this is a dashboard, this is my top level KPIs for this particular ad account, is that I want to see at the high level how things are performing. Now you'll know that when I'm looking at top level numbers, I'm drilling down. So return on ad spend is a factor of my cost per purchase, cost per acquisition, and my average order value. Now, one of the downsides right now in Ad Manager is that you can't bring custom metrics in. So I would love to have average order value here, so I can see which campaigns are driving higher value and which are not, which I can't, so that's fine. But again, I can look at my benchmark. So I look at 22.86 as my average cost per purchase. Where am I underperforming? Where am I overperforming? So this one here, for example, and here's something quite interesting. The cost per purchase is $32, and I'm getting a 1.6 ROAS, whereas here, my cost per purchase is higher, and I'm getting a low ROAS,

## [06:03 - 09:03]

and I'm just scanning down, and this one here. So my cost per purchase is $11, I've got four here. I'm looking here, and what I'm trying to do is identify where the opportunities are to optimize. What I would do is I'd be scanning across by layer. So for example, I wouldn't look at the whole funnel, I'd look at my cold audiences first, and it's really easy to type in here and kind of just look for cold, for example. So if I now list my cold audiences, I then look at amounts spent, I can now see what my cold return on ad spend is. So for this ad account, it's 1.29 ROAS, and all it says is campaign name is cold, and I've got more than one impressions. I can see the same for warm. So for my middle layer, I can then look at ROAS here. So I can see that my middle layer is 2.28, and again, what I would do in my analysis is to find issues and opportunities. So for example, this one here is a low spend, but it's underperforming on my ROAS, whereas this one here has the highest spend, it's good, it's on 2.25, but this one here are promotional DCO, which was a short-term campaign, did even better. So I'm looking for those opportunities, because if I see an opportunity where ROAS is lower than it should be, I'll drill down, and I'll keep drilling down to the ad set and ad level to see where the opportunities are, and I can do the same for the different layers. So this is an idea, this is to give you an idea of how I would set up certain dashboards. This is my top level KPIs for E-com, and then showing you how to do analysis to spot opportunities. So again, you can look at CPA, you can look at other metrics, but this is the only thing I'm interested in right now for this ad account is I have a 4.69 ROAS, how can I get better value out of it? And don't forget that the devil is in the details. So for example, I might have a 4.71 ROAS here, but if I click into this, maybe my ad sets tell me a different story. And again, look at the averages. So the average for this one is 4.72 ROAS. Why is this one doing 6.23? Why is this one doing 9.24? Why are we spending so much here? Yet we're making a lower ROAS. Why are we spending here and make a lower ROAS? This gives me an idea of where to optimize. So we'll cover off the optimization section separately, but this is why I set up my dashboards in a certain way. And just to reiterate here, the way I create this is I would go into my customization and you can do this as well. I want to customize my columns based on this. So I want to see budget, amount spent, ROAS, cost per purchase, result rate, which is conversion rate, cost per outbound click, purchase, conversion value and purchases. So that's at the highest level. And that's just one view. You can create many more here

## [09:03 - 09:59]

and it then allows you to create smaller views of your data. One thing I do is I mostly set up these kind of smaller number of metrics. Sometimes I go into an ad account and I just set every column I ever need. So some of you will absolutely relate to this. And if I click into main KPIs here, there's a long list of KPIs and it scrolls and scrolls and scrolls because I know what data I want to see and I want to be able to see in one glance. So you have that opportunity as well. So if I go to customize columns here, you'll see a long list of metrics which are basically everything that I want to see. And it gives me an opportunity to see everything at once. But for most people, it makes sense to break them into smaller parts. Just focus on the metrics that make most sense for you and then learn how to interpret that data.
